_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Respiratory: Mechanical Ventilation (Invasive). NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Respiratory: Mechanical Ventilation (Invasive)

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Respiratory: Mechanical Ventilation (Invasive)
points: Maintain adequate oxygenation
Maintain adequate minute ventilation and capnography targets based on patient pathophysiology
Prevent or limit risk of short- and long-term invasive airway and ventilator-associated complications including barotrauma, pneumothorax, aspiration, over-ventilation

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Adult patients with invasive airway requiring mechanical ventilation
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Interfacility transfer patients with established vent settings
Patients with suspected untreated pneumothorax or large airway injury
Patients in cardiac arrest

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
CPR/AED if patient becomes pulseless
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
CPAP for respiratory distress
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain
Albuterol SVN for bronchospasm component
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV/IO fluid resuscitation as indicated
Waveform capnography
IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol
IV antiemetics (ondansetron) per protocol
IV D50W/D10W for hypoglycemia

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation
Endotracheal intubation for airway compromise
RSI per protocol
Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol
All parenteral medications per protocol
Vasopressors for refractory hemodynamic compromise

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Ventilators have different capabilities and features. Users must be familiar with the device they use and must be properly educated on its use and application in the specific population being treated

Ensure that all vent alarms are set appropriately, and patient is continually monitored with pulse oximetry and waveform capnography a. Set all alarms that involve high pressure, low pressure, minute volume, and apnea
During transport of a critically ill patient only necessary adjustments should be made to the ventilator. Focus on maintaining adequate oxygenation, minute volume and patient comfort.

An increase in the respiratory rate shortens the expiratory time. If changing rate, also check the I:E ratio (the proportions of each breath cycle devoted to the inspiratory and expiratory phases) and adjust the inspiratory time if necessary

The inspiratory time can be adjusted slightly to ensure greater patient comfort, however any change in inspiratory time will affect the I:E ratio. Rarely should an inspiratory time be less than 0.7 for an adult
Assure proper sedation level for patient to tolerate ventilator

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: It is important to understand the patient's underlying pulmonary status to choose the appropriate type of ventilation (volume or pressure) and mode (AC or SIMV most common) a. Volume control ventilation is generally preferred initially in adults with compliant lungs (PPlat less than 30) because of better control of minute ventilation
Perform a pre-ventilator use inspection including a circuit check on the ventilator prior to placing it on a patient
Assess values during transport, including: a. Peak inspiratory pressure (PIP) Compare against baseline value to monitor for compliance changes or obstruction in the circuit

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: Ventilator Settings: Volume or pressure breaths; mode; respiratory rate; inspiratory time; tidal volume or pressure, PEEP, FiO2; sensitivity
Patient Values (baseline and repeated): Peak inspiratory pressure (PIP); exhaled tidal volume; respiratory rate; I:E ratio; minute volume; EtCO2; SPO2

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: During transport of a critically ill patient only necessary adjustments should be made to the ventilator. Focus on maintaining adequate oxygenation, minute volume and patient comfort.
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
